questions.prompt: Why does RTL push you toward getByRole over getByTestId?

choices.text: getByRole matches how assistive tech (screen readers, keyboard nav) finds elements — tests pass when the UI is genuinely accessible. data-testid is an escape hatch for cases where no semantic option works; relying on it lets tests pass while users with disabilities can't use the feature. The query priority (role → label → text → testid) reflects how visible each path is to real users. Tests resemble usage; usage stays accessible.

questions.explanation: getByRole matches how assistive tech navigates — keeps tests accessible-aligned.

questions.prompt: Codebase has 200 components in one /components folder. New engineers can't find anything. Fix?

choices.text: Switch to feature-first structure: features/auth/, features/posts/, features/dashboard/, each containing the components/hooks/api/types for that domain. Adding a feature = adding a folder; removing = removing a folder. Each feature exposes a public API via index.ts; other features import from there, not directly into internals. Shared UI primitives stay in components/ui/. Migrate incrementally — one feature at a time. The structural shift dramatically improves navigability and limits cross-feature coupling.

questions.explanation: Feature-first folders + public API per feature. Migrate incrementally.

choices.text: Fully controlled with useState per field.

React Hook Form + Zod. RHF uses uncontrolled (ref-based) inputs — each keystroke doesn't re-render the whole form, dramatically faster for forms with 20+ fields. Zod centralizes validation in a schema; `z.infer<typeof schema>` derives the form's TypeScript types. Same schema runs server-side (Server Actions, API routes), so client and server share one validation source of truth. Less boilerplate, fewer re-renders, type safety end-to-end. The 2026 default.

questions.explanation: RHF + Zod: uncontrolled inputs, schema validation, shared client/server rules.

questions.prompt: Form has 30 fields, sluggish on every keystroke. Diagnostic?

choices.text: Likely fully-controlled with useState per field — each keystroke re-renders the whole form, all 30 fields go through render. Switch to React Hook Form: inputs become ref-based, only individual fields re-render, the form root only re-renders when validation state changes. Also audit `watch()` calls — every watch causes re-renders on its field changes; use only when one field genuinely gates another's render. The shift typically delivers a 5-10x perf improvement on form interactions.

questions.explanation: Controlled-useState forms re-render fully per keystroke. RHF's ref-based approach fixes it.

questions.prompt: Building a custom dropdown. A11y approach?

choices.text: Use Radix UI, React Aria, or shadcn/ui's accessible dropdown primitives. Building keyboard navigation (arrow keys, Home/End, type-ahead), focus management (move into menu on open, restore on close), correct ARIA (aria-expanded, aria-controls, aria-haspopup), and screen reader announcements correctly from scratch is hard — easy to get 80% there and miss edge cases that exclude users. Libraries get this right after years of refinement; you style on top. Strongly prefer accessible primitives over hand-rolling for complex widgets like dropdowns, dialogs, comboboxes, date pickers.

questions.explanation: Use accessible primitives (Radix, React Aria) for complex widgets. Don't hand-roll keyboard/ARIA.
